clean_story:
Stone to silicon.  Hands extend with tools. "Tool evolution" traces how humans turned raw materials into extensions of their bodies—first for survival, then for precision. A sharpened rock isn’t just a cutter; it’s a stored idea, passed down and improved.
Early tools were brute-force multipliers:a stick digs faster than fingers. A lever lifts what arms can’t. But the real shift came when tools started remembering skill—like a bow’s curve holding the knowledge of past hunts.
Metals changed everything. Copper axes (3000 BCE) stayed sharp longer than flint. Iron plows (500 BCE) turned heavy soil into farmland, feeding cities. Each upgrade wasn’t just stronger—it demanded less human effort per result.
Then came tools that think:A 19th-century loom’s punch cards encoded patterns like binary code. Today’s CNC machines carve metal guided by software, repeating perfect cuts without tiring. The hand didn’t get stronger—it got smarter.
Three related subjects are skill inheritance, energy tradeoffs and tool miniaturization.
